assignment_description:
Introduction
⍞ Materials: Atlas, Globe or Wall Map showing North America, My Canada, geography journal

PREP: Print Blank World Map, Blank North America Map, Blank Outline Map of Canada.
∞ Map Link: Blank World Map
∞ Map Link: Blank North America Map
∞ Map Link: Blank Outline Map of Canada (download the first option under "Maps of Canada")

➜ INTRO
In geography this year, we will begin a two-year journey across Canada. Geography is learning about different places around the world. All kinds of maps are used to help us in the study of geography—globes, wall maps, and atlases.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Atlas, Globe, or Wall Map showing North America
1\. Find North America.
2\. Find the equator. Is Canada north or south of the equator?
3\. What is to the west of Canada? (Pacific Ocean) To the east? (Atlantic Ocean) To the north? (Arctic Ocean) To the south? (United States of America)

➜ MAP WORK
Add the Blank World Map (referred to moving forward as "My World Map"), the Blank North America Map (referred to moving forward as "My North America Map"), and the Blank Outline Map of Canada (referred to moving forward as "My Canada Map") to your geography journal.

⍞ Map Resource: My World Map
1\. Add the title "My World Map."
2\. Colour in Canada.

⍞ Map Resource: My North America Map
1\. Add title "My North America Map."
2\. Label the borders of Canada. (Pacific Ocean, Atlantic Ocean, Arctic Ocean, United States of America)
3\. Label any other countries of interest, using a North American map or atlas as a reference (Greenland, Mexico, etc.).

➜ READ & NARRATE
My Canada
"Oh, Canada" - "polar bears."

• Which of these facts were new to you? What are you most excited to learn about Canada? The parent/teacher or the student (depending on ability) could write these down in the geography journal.

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Depending on where you live, it may rain often or not much at all. Please start to take notes on where the water goes when it rains. Look for where it goes near your home, and even see if you can follow it in your neighbourhood. The notes can go in your nature or geography journal.

Canada
⍞ Materials: My Canada; The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

PREP: Print the flag of Canada and Canada's Coat of Arms.
∞ Image Link: Colouring Book of Flags: Canada
∞ Image Link: Canada's Coat of Arms

➜ INTRO
Let's look at the whole map of Canada at the beginning of My Canada. What do you notice?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Run your finger along the southern border of Canada. Most of Canada's population lives near this border. Can you name some of the cities you see there?
2\. Locate the capital city of Canada (Ottawa). Discuss what it means to be a capital city, and give some examples of other capital cities around the world.
3\. Locate your province or territory. How close do you live to the southern border? How close do you live to the capital city of Ottawa? Could you drive there, or would you have to fly?
4\. Looking at the map, point out places you've visited or places where your family and friends live.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Canada Map
1\. Add title "My Canada Map."
2\. Add Ottawa (with a star inside a circle) to the map and label it.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
The Kids Book of Canada
p.4 "Canada's name" - "February 15, 1965."

• Facts about countries can change over time. Look up the current population of Canada and compare it to the number in the "Quick Facts" box. What might be some reasons that the population of Canada is getting bigger?

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Colour the flag of Canada and add it to your geography journal.
2\. Colour Canada's Coat of Arms and add it to your geography journal. Spend some time looking at all the figures there. Did you know Canada's Coat of Arms has a unicorn?

• EXTRA HELPING
Watch the Heritage Minute about the naming of Canada
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Jacques Cartier (1:00)

Compass
⍞ Materials: compass, paper, tape

➜ DISCUSS
There are naturally occurring magnetic rocks called magnetite, but modern compasses use a magnetized needle. Both magnetite and a modern magnetic needle, when allowed to move freely, will swing to point north. Today, we will use a compass to find direction in your classroom or room in your home.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Place the compass in the middle of the room and allow it to stop moving.
2\. The needle will be pointing north. You must move the N sign on the compass to line up with the point of the needle.
3\. On a piece of paper, write N for north and tape it to the wall that the compass is pointing to.
4\. When you face north, east will be on your right, west on your left, and south directly behind you.
5\. Using these directions, make more sheets with E, W, and S on them and tape them to your walls.

➜ PLAY
Using the directions on the walls, take turns with another student or teacher, following directions such as "take 3 steps east" or "take 2 giant steps south." You can even make it into a Mother May I game.

Compass Rose
⍞ Materials: compass, chalk

➜ DISCUSS
During the last lesson, we found direction inside, and today we will find direction outside and create a compass rose.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Draw a large circle on a paved area. Place the compass in the middle and allow it to stop moving.
2\. The needle will be pointing north. You must move the N sign on the compass to line up with the point of the needle.
3\. Draw a line from the N side of the compass straight to the edge of the circle, and mark it with an N.
4\. Do the same thing with E, W, and S.
5\. Today, we will add more directions to the compass rose. Halfway between the N and the E, you will write a NE, which stands for northeast, on the circle. Halfway between N and W, you will write NW, which stands for northwest. Continue by adding a SE between S and E, for southeast, and add SW between S and W, for southwest.
6\. You will notice that it is easier to give more accurate directions because you have more to refer to.

➜ PLAY
1\. Using the directions on the compass rose, take turns with another student or teacher, following directions such as "take 3 steps northeast" or "take 2 giant steps southwest." This can be played like Mother May I.
2\. Another game you can play is to give directions to an object, like a tree, outside by saying things like "take 5 steps west, and then 2 steps northwest. What is there?"

Compass Rose and Map Key
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-7 "You've moved" - "these tools!"

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Making Your Own Maps, various pages
1\. Can you find the compass rose on p.7? Which directions are on it?
2\. Locate the keys on p.5 and 7. What symbols do they use? How are they the same or different?
3\. Look at the keys on maps on p.15,16,19, 21. How are they the same or different?

Physical Geography & Biomes
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada

➜ INTRO
As we will see, there are lots of different types of maps, and maps can show many different types of information. The map in My Canada we've been looking at for the past few weeks is a political map: it gives information about borders, boundaries, capital cities, and names. But it doesn't tell us much about what the land is like, what the temperature is, or what animals and plants we might find.

➜ READ & NARRATE
p.5 "Taiga landscape" - "in the valleys."

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. How many different biomes can be found in Canada? (If you haven't studied biomes before, spend some extra time reading the descriptions in the book, or do some additional research about the characteristics of each.)
2\. What biome do you live in? What is the climate like throughout the year, and what is it like right now? What plants and animals are found in your area?
3\. How has the mapmaker chosen to represent what can be found in Canada? What images do you see? (When a map shows the landforms, natural regions, climate, and biomes of an area, it is an example of physical geography.)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Go for a walk in your neighbourhood. What grows near you? What type of vegetation (trees, bushes, flowers, etc.) grows near you? What is the climate like right now? You could add this information to your Outdoor Work notebook.

Types of Maps Intro

➜ INTRO
Last week, we looked at a map that showed different aspects of Canada's physical geography. We are going to spend a few lessons learning about different types of maps.

➜ DISCUSS
1\. Today, we will look at two more types of maps. The first we will call a model or a 3-dimensional map. Last year, you used blocks (or some other objects) to make maps. You did this with your room, your home, your neighbours, etc.
2\. The second type of map we will call a pictorial view. It is a drawing of a street-level view of a home, building, etc. You also drew these last year when you drew your home and neighbouring homes/buildings.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the linked map pictures.
1\. Look at the linked Room Model and Model Showing Orientation.
∞ Image link: Room Model
∞ Image link: Model Showing Orientation
2\. Make a quick model of a room in your home or school. If you have previously done this activity, choose a different room to model.
3\. Look at the linked pictorial view of a house. It will be at eye level, and drawn as if you are looking directly at it.
∞ Image link: Pictorial map
4\. If time permits, draw a pictorial view of your home from the outside.

Types of Maps, Cont.
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ RECAP
What types of maps did we discuss in the last lesson?

➜ DISCUSS
1\. Today, we will look at the next 2 types of maps. The first we will call a panoramic 3D map. Imagine you are a bird high up in a tree looking down at your home; what kind of drawing would that make? How would it look different from the street view of your home?
2\. The second type of map we will call an aerial view. This time, imagine you are a bird flying directly over your home; what kind of drawing would that make? (could only see the roof) How is this different from the panoramic view?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the linked map pictures, as well as one in Making Your Own Maps.
1\. Look at the linked Panoramic 3D map and have the student identify its type.
∞ Map Link: Panoramic 3D map
2\. Look at the linked Aerial View map, and have the student identify its type.
∞ Map Link: Aerial View map
3\. Look at Making Your Own Maps p.16. What type of map is this, based on what we studied today? (panoramic)
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
If your student(s) has used Making Your Own Maps before, have them try to draw a panoramic 3D or an aerial view map of an area familiar to them.

assignment_description:
Types of Maps, Cont.
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps

➜ RECAP
What types of maps did we discuss in the last lesson?

➜ DISCUSS
Today, we will look at the last type of map we will be discussing this year. This type of map is a symbolic map. These can vary greatly. Making Your Own Maps is full of symbolic maps. As you read today, look for maps that are not models, pictorial, panoramic, or aerial.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.4-15 "You've moved" - "stands for."

➜ DISCUSS
Did you find symbol maps? (p.5, 7, 8, 11, 13, 15) Note: If there is confusion about why some of those are not pictorial or panoramic, look back at the previous 2 lessons.

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Don't forget to keep an eye out when it rains. Try to pay attention to a light rain versus a hard rain. Don't forget to keep notes in your geography or nature journal.

People, Places, and Events
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.6-7 "On November 7, 1885" - "markets for its goods."

• Discuss these events, the ones your student(s) know and the ones that might be new.

➜ ILLUSTRATE
Draw or find images of the events of most interest to add to your Picture Timeline. Be sure to add when your own province or territory joined Canada.

Compass Rose and Directions
⍞ Materials: My Canada, My Map of Canada; geography journal

➜ INTRO
We have talked about directions in your room/classroom and outdoors. Today, we will learn about directions on a map.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Find the compass rose on the map of Canada at the beginning of My Canada
2\. What direction is north? South? East? West?
4\. What is to the west of British Columbia? (Pacific Ocean)
5\. What ocean is east of Ontario? (Atlantic Ocean)
6\. If we travel from Victoria to Halifax, which direction would you travel? (east)
7\. Where do you live? Which direction would you go to get to Ottawa?

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Add a compass rose to My Map of Canada.

Islands
⍞ Materials: World Wall Map or Globe

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. Locate your home on the globe.
2\. Locate the Equator. Do you live north or south of it?
3\. Which continent do you live on?
4\. Which ocean is closest to you?
5\. What is an island? (A body of land surrounded by water.)
6\. Locate a very large island in the Atlantic Ocean. (The largest island is Greenland, which extends from the Arctic Ocean into the North Atlantic Ocean. Other islands are listed in the next question.)
7\. Can you locate any more islands in the Atlantic Ocean? (Possibilities include Iceland, Ireland, Great Britain, Newfoundland, and the Falklands, among others.)
8\. Some islands in the Pacific Ocean are isolated, while others exist in groups. Can you locate a group of islands in the Pacific Ocean? (Aleutian Islands, Hawaii, Galápagos Islands, Japan, Philippine Islands, Northern Mariana Islands, Marshall Islands, Micronesia, Solomon Islands, Cook Islands, French Polynesia, and New Zealand, among many others)
9\. Can you locate standalone islands in the Pacific that are not part of a group? (This question is more of a scavenger hunt, and the student may only name Vancouver Island and Taiwan. Searching for individual islands in the Pacific Ocean makes it apparent that almost all islands in this ocean are part of a larger group.)
10\. What is an island close to you?
11\. How many islands can you find on the east coast of Canada?

Vancouver Island and British Columbia
⍞ Materials: My Canada, The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

PREP: (Optional) Print off a blank map of British Columbia (see the teacher's note).
∞ Map Link: Outline Maps of Canada and Provinces (these maps have rivers and lakes)
∞ Map Link: Completely Blank Map of British Columbia

➜ INTRO
Charlotte Mason defined an island as follows in her book, Elementary Geography. "The high parts of the ocean bed rise above water in many places in the Pacific. These are called islands. An island is just a piece of land with water all around it. On the map, you will find many islands scattered over the Pacific Ocean. They are mostly small and lie in groups; that is, several clustered together."

Have you ever been to an island? If you lived on an island, what are some things you could do for fun?

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: Kids Book of Canada, p.9 (Today, we are only looking at the islands on the west coast of British Columbia.)
1\. Locate Victoria, which is the capital of BC. What kind of landform is it on? (Vancouver Island)
2\. What body of water is to the west of British Columbia? (Pacific Ocean)
3\. Are there other islands on the west coast? (Queen Charlotte Islands, now called Haida Gwaii)
4\. From the city of Vancouver, in which direction do you travel to get to Victoria? (south)
5\. From the city of Vancouver, in which direction do you travel to get to Nanaimo? (west)
6\. How could you travel from the mainland of British Columbia to the islands off its coast? (ferry or seaplane)

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
⍞ Map Resource: My Canada (map of British Columbia)
Look at the two inset maps at the bottom about Vancouver Island. What places do you see there, and what activities might you do?

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
Mark Victoria with a star to show it is a capital city.

• EXTRA HELPING
Emily Carr (1871-1945) was a Canadian artist who found inspiration in the beauty of Vancouver Island.
∞ Link: Emily Carr
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
For students who have an extra interest in mapping, there will be an optional blank provincial or territorial map provided. Students can decide whether to do all their map work on "My Map of Canada," or, if they want to add extra details and illustrations, they may wish to have one map for each province/territory.

★ TEACHER TIP
Lesson plans will include some Extra Helping links and suggestions connected to the places on the map. However, as the students spend time looking at the images in My Canada, take some additional time to look up any of the places, animals, natural features, or landmarks that are of interest.

assignment_description:
British Columbia
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

➜ INTRO
We are going to spend some time exploring the rest of British Columbia.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: The Kids Book of Canada "Canada" p.4-5
1\. Find the province of British Columbia on the map. What does it border? (Yukon, Northwest Territories, Alberta, and the USA)
2\. What are the crinkly lines that run north-south through the province? (Mountains)
3\. How many different geographical regions do we find in British Columbia? Each one has a number and a separate colour. (There are four: Pacific Coast, Western Cordillera, Boreal Forest, and Taiga.)
4\. From these regions, what might the climate be like in different parts of BC?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-9 "British Columbia" - "dry, sunny slopes."

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: The Kids Book of Canada p.9
1\. What is the highest peak in the Rocky Mountains? (Mount Robson) Can you find it on the map?
2\. Follow the route of the Fraser River from the foot of the Rocky Mountains to the Pacific Ocean. Why do you think it turns the way it does? (Rivers can't flow over the mountain ranges.)

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Label British Columbia.
2\. Label Vancouver. While it isn't the capital of BC, it is one of Canada's largest cities.
3\. Draw in the mountains and label Mount Robson.
4\. Draw in and label any other features or illustrations of interest (bodies of water, cities, landmarks, natural features, etc.).

• EXTRA HELPING
All blank provincial and territorial flags can be found at the link below.
∞ Image Link: Colouring Book of Flags: Canada
assignment_teachersNote:
★ TEACHER NOTE
There are many options for how students can record their learning about each province and territory in their geography journal. Some students might want to record relevant facts (population, size, motto, capital, provincial symbols, etc.); others may want to spend extra time mapping rivers, mountains, and cities on blank maps; still others may wish to colour blank flags and draw pictures. Choose what would appeal most to your individual student(s), and extra work could be done during afternoon occupations.

assignment_description:
British Columbia, cont.
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

PREP: Print the flag of British Columbia.
∞ Image Link: Colouring Book of Flags: Canada

➜ RECAP
What did you learn about British Columbia in the previous lesson?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.10-11 "At Barkerville" - "other natural resources."
• Spend some time today entering an item of interest into your Picture Timeline. Students could also continue to decorate their map of British Columbia. Students who are particularly interested in these activities can continue this work in the afternoons.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
As we learn about each province and territory, we will also study some animals that are found only in Canada. We will read the book Canada Wild out of order as we learn about Canada from west to east.
p.3 "Introduction" - "keep them safe."
• The first endemic animal we will read about is from the coastal region of British Columbia.
p.14-17 "Habitat: Coastal rainforests - "Kermode bear."
• Can you find the Kermode Bear on the British Columbia map in My Canada?

➜ MAP WORK
Colour the flag of British Columbia and add it to your geography journal.

• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about Terry Fox.
∞ Video Link: Why Terry Fox is a National Hero

British Columbia, cont.
⍞ Materials: My Canada; Canada Wild

➜ RECAP
What did you learn about British Columbia in the previous lesson?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
British Columbia map in My Canada (pages not numbered)

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. What are some of the animals that live in British Columbia? How many different ones can you find?
2\. Can you find a National Park in BC? What about some of the Provincial Parks?
3\. Looking at the images, what are some activities that people might like to do in British Columbia?
4\. In the last lesson, you read about the gold rush in Barkerville. Can you find it on the map?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.30-33 "Pacific Steller's Jay" - "keep its numbers strong."

• EXTRA HELPING
Buy some Nanaimo Bars for a treat, or make your own!
∞ Link: Nanaimo Bar Recipe

Alberta
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

PREP: (Optional) Print off a blank map of Alberta for more detailed mapping.
∞ Map Link: Outline Maps of Canada and Provinces (these maps have rivers and lakes)
∞ Map Link: Completely Blank Map of Alberta

➜ INTRO
The next two weeks will be spent learning about the province of Alberta.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: The Kids Book of Canada "Canada" p.4-5
1\. Find the province of Alberta on the map. What does it border? (British Columbia, Northwest Territories, Saskatchewan, and the USA)
2\. What landform does it share with British Columbia, along its western border? (Rocky Mountains)
3\. How many different geographical regions do we find in Alberta? Each one has a number and a separate colour. (There are four: the Western Cordillera, the Interior Plains, the Boreal Forest, and the Taiga.)
4\. From these regions, what might the climate be like in different parts of Alberta?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.12-13 "Alberta was named" - "towers called hoodoos."

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
1\. Label Alberta.
2\. Label Edmonton with a star to show it is a capital city.
3\. Draw in the mountains and label Mount Columbia.
4\. Draw in and label any other features or illustrations of interest (bodies of water, cities, landmarks, natural features, etc.).

• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about Hoodoos.
∞ Article Link: Hoodoos of Drumheller

See images from Wood Buffalo National Park
∞ Video Link: Highlights of Wood Buffalo National Park

Explore Jasper National Park
∞ Video Link: Jasper National Park

Alberta, cont.
⍞ Materials: The Kids Book of Canada

➜ RECAP
What did you learn about Alberta in the previous lesson?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.14-15 "Horses weren't used" - "hurt the environment."

• Spend some time today entering an item of interest into your Picture Timeline. Students could also continue to decorate their map of Alberta.

• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about Emily Murphy.
∞ Video Link: Heritage Minutes: Emily Murphy (1:02)

Alberta, cont.
⍞ Materials: My Canada; Canada Wild

➜ RECAP
What did you learn about Alberta in the previous lesson?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Alberta map in My Canada (pages not numbered)

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
1\. What are some of the animals that live in Alberta? How many different ones can you find?
2\. Can you find any National Parks in Alberta? What about some of the Provincial Parks?
3\. Looking at the images, what are some activities that people might like to do in Alberta?
4\. Each map in this book has a symbol in the corners - can you find Alberta's? Do you know what that is, and why it might have been chosen? (Horseshoe; Calgary Stampede and ranching)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.6-9 "Whooping Crane" - "through the season."

➜ ILLUSTRATE (optional)
Draw a whooping crane using watercolours or another medium such as chalk, pastels, or crayons.

• EXTRA HELPING
Learn more about the World's Largest Pysanka (Ukrainian painted Easter egg) in Vegreville, Alberta.
∞ Article Link: Vegreville
∞ Video Link: Whooping Cranes in Wood Buffalo National Park

Alberta, cont.
⍞ Materials: My Canada, The Kids Book of Canada; geography journal

PREP: Print the flag of Alberta.
∞ Image Link: Colouring Book of Flags: Canada

➜ MAP WORK
Colour the flag of Alberta and add it to your geography journal.

➜ MAPPING ACTIVITY
Spend today's lesson learning more about Alberta: check out some Extra Helpings from previous lessons, look up additional images, or add some more detail to your maps. The rivers in Alberta are especially interesting to trace!
If you live in Alberta, today would be a great day to check out a local landmark or take a nature walk.

Globe
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ INTRO
Today, we will go over some information that you may already know, but enjoy it as a review if you do.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: globe
1\. Imagine a ball for the first few questions.
2\. Does a ball have a top and a bottom? (Yes, but you can easily turn it to change what is "top" and "bottom")
3\. Does it have an end? (No.)
4\. Are you able to see all of the ball at one time? (No, because you can only see the part facing you.)
5\. Is there any way to see all of it at once? (No. If you turn it in any direction, there is still a part you can’t see.)
6\. Now look at the globe. How does the globe compare to a ball? How are they the same? Different?
7\. Are you able to see all parts of the globe at once?
8\. What do you already know about the globe?
9\. Spend some time simply looking at your globe and talking about what you see.

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Near the beginning of term 2, we will have some lessons about how water moves. Try to pay attention to where water stays after the rain, where it pools. Don't forget to keep notes in your geography or nature journal.

Map Work
⍞ Materials: Globe or World Wall Map

➜ INTRO
Today, we will go over some information that you may already know, but enjoy it as a review if you do.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: globe or world wall map
1\. Locate the Equator.
2\. Where is the Northern Hemisphere? Southern Hemisphere?
3\. Where is the North Pole? South Pole?
4\. We are going to locate the 7 continents. Locate North America, South America, Europe, Asia, Australia, Africa, and Antarctica.
5\. We are going to locate the 4 (or 5) named oceans of the world. Locate the Atlantic Ocean.
6\. Locate the Pacific Ocean.
7\. Locate the Indian Ocean.
8\. Locate the Arctic Ocean.
9\. Locate the Southern Ocean. (National Geographic atlases do not recognize the Southern Ocean as its own distinct ocean.)
10\. Some people divide the Pacific and Atlantic Oceans into northern and southern parts. Where is the North Atlantic Ocean? South Atlantic Ocean? North Pacific Ocean? South Pacific Ocean?
11\. Which ocean is the northernmost? (Arctic) The southernmost? (Southern)
12\. Which continent is the largest? (Asia) The smallest? (Australia)
13\. Which ocean is the largest? (Pacific) The smallest? (Arctic)
14\. Locate the Atlantic Ocean. It is bounded on the east by which continent(s)? (Europe and Africa.)
15\. The Atlantic Ocean is bounded on the west by which continent(s)? (North and South America.)
16\. Locate the Pacific Ocean. Which continent(s) borders the Pacific Ocean to the east? (North and South America.) On the west? (Asia and Australia.)
17\. Locate the Indian Ocean. Which continent(s) border the Indian Ocean on the east? (Asia and Australia.) On the west? (Africa) On the north? (Asia)
18\. Locate the Arctic Ocean, and identify which continent(s) it borders. (North America, Europe, and Asia.)
19\. Locate the area of water known as the Southern Ocean and identify which continent(s) it borders. (Antarctica. Some maps from Australia consider the Southern Ocean extending as far north as its southern coast.)

Exam Week

➜ EXAMS
Answer question(s) related to the course.

Questions will come from:
The Kids Book of Canada
My Canada
assignment_teachersNote:
★ Geography: Grade 2 (Canada) — TERM 1 EXAM QUESTIONS
· My Canada: Imagine you are taking a trip to Vancouver Island from another part of Canada. How would you get there? What kinds of things would you expect to see? What are some things that make islands special?

· The Kids Book of Canada: Tell what you remember about Alberta. What different regions are there, and what are some of the animals that live there?

assignment_description:
⍞ Materials: Map of Your Street

PREP: Map: Google Maps (Street View), Google Earth, Bing Maps (Streetside), a map application, or some other image that will allow an eye-level view of your home. This will be used during this lesson and the next.

➜ INTRO
Can you see your street on a globe or in an atlas? (No, the maps have a large scale.) Today we will be able to see your street and even your home.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
⍞ Map Resource: “Your street and house” (Use a phone or computer to view a street-level image.)
1\. Find your home. (This is a good time to learn your home address.)
2\. Can you see your neighbour’s home? Your street?
3\. Zoom out. What happens to the image? What changes? Can you see your home?
4\. Zoom out enough for it to look similar to your province/territory map in the atlas.
5\. Zoom bit by bit all the way back in. What happens?
6\. Why do you think your house/street is not on the atlas map?

➜ NOTE
Next week, you will need a printed copy of the street view of this in order to do the pet map of the neighbourhood.
You can also use time in the afternoons this week, interviewing neighbours about their pets (what types and how many) to be added to a map next week.

Pet Map

PREP: Print the Google Maps (Street View), Google Earth, Bing Maps (Streetside), a map application, or some other image that will allow a view of your immediate neighbourhood. If you live in an apt building, it might be easier to print a photo of the front of the building and note which apartments are which.

➜ INTRO
It would be fun to see what pets live in your neighbourhood and to add them to your Google Map you explored last week. If you have not already, interview neighbours about what types of pets they have and how many.

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: Google Map of neighbourhood
1\. Create a key showing symbols for each type of pet.
2\. Add the symbols to the neighbours' houses/apartments on your map.
3\. If there is more than 1 pet per residence, add multiple symbols to each residence.
4\. If you would like, share your map with a family member or friend and tell them what you learned about your neighbours.

Latitude and Longitude
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ INTRO
We are going to spend some time today learning more about the lines on your globe. This may be a review for your student.

➜ MAP WORK
• Latitude
1\. Locate the Equator, Tropic of Capricorn, and Tropic of Cancer.
2\. Locate 2 more named lines of latitude. (Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle)
3\. Is the Arctic Circle located in the Northern or Southern Hemisphere? (Northern)
4\. Which other major circle of latitude is located in the Northern Hemisphere? (Tropic of Cancer)
5\. Which 2 major circles of latitude are located in the Southern Hemisphere? (Antarctic Circle and Tropic of Capricorn.)
6\. Name the continents that touch the Arctic Circle. (North America, Europe, and Asia.) The Antarctic Circle? (Antarctica)
• Longitude
1\. Locate the prime meridian. (look in England)
2\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both North and South America. (roughly between 35°W and 81°W)
3\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both Asia and Australia. (roughly between 114°E and 153°E)
4\. Name a line of longitude that goes through both Europe and Africa. (roughly between 17°W and 52°E)
5\. Can you locate a line of longitude that passes through Antarctica but no other continents? (No)

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Next week, we will have our lessons on how water moves. Make sure you have your notes ready from previous rain walks.

Map Projections
⍞ Materials: Globe, World Map; My Canada

➜ INTRO
We are going to compare your globe to your wall map of the world.

➜ MAP WORK
1\. Locate the 7 continents on your globe and then on the wall map.
2\. Compare the sizes of each continent on the globe. Now compare the sizes of each continent on the wall map. Are there any differences in the relative sizes depicted on the globe versus the sizes depicted on the wall map? (For example, Africa is the 2nd largest continent on the globe. However, North America appears to be the 2nd largest continent on the wall map.)
3\. What do you notice about the differences between Antarctica? (Antarctica appears stretched on the wall map.)
4\. Are there any differences in the sizes of the oceans? (The Arctic and Southern Oceans appear larger on the wall map than their depiction on the globe.)
5\. Practical question: If you wanted to peel an orange and lay the peel flat, how could you do that? What would it look like?

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
1\. Watch the following Map Projections video on YouTube.
∞ Video Link: Map Projections video (6:00)
2\. Using your globe and wall map, spend the rest of your time comparing continents and countries on each. What are the biggest differences?
3\. Which projection is your wall map? (Reference the fine print on the map.)

• EXTRA HELPING
This activity helpfully demonstrates why map projections are necessary. If you would like to follow along with the demonstration on the video, you'll need your own orange, permanent marker, and knife.
∞ Video Link: Orange Globe

Water Moves

PREP: This is the lesson where you will use information you have gathered while noticing what happens around your home during rain.

➜ INTRO
We have found some of the large bodies of water on our globe, the oceans. We will locate other bodies of water later, but let's just focus on water today and how it moves. Have you been paying attention to where water goes when it rains near your home?

➜ DISCUSS
1\. What do you notice when it rains? Is there a difference when it rains lightly versus heavily? Is there a difference when it is a quick shower versus rain that lasts many hours or even days?
2\. When the rain falls on the roof of your home, where does it go? (gutters, straight off the sides, etc.)
3\. Is there any place near your home where you know you will find a puddle after the rain?
4\. Why do you think water stays in that spot? Is it a big area? (You should notice high and low places and that water runs to the low places.)
5\. Is there anything else you have noticed about how water moves near your home?
6\. Do you water plants at your home? What happens when you water them? Is there a difference in watering a plant in a pot versus in the ground? How often do you have to water plants/grass outside where you live?

➜ DRAW
1\. On a piece of blank paper, the teacher can draw a general outline of the school/home from above.
2\. Have the student draw some of the places that water moves around the home/school, noting places where the water puddles. These are the low places.

Geography Model
⍞ Materials: something large and flat, either a cookie sheet or a cutting board; clay, sink, water source

➜ INTRO
Today, we will watch water move on a small scale.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Tilt the flat surface into the sink so that the outside end is 1 inch higher than the end in the sink.
2\. Pour water slowly onto the raised end and note what it does.
3\. Raise the outside end to about 3 inches, and then 6 inches, pouring water slowly both times at the same spot. Do you notice any differences? (water moves faster the higher it starts)
4\. Lay the surface flat, dry it, and build a few mountains in the center of it, making a place for a "river" to run.
5\. Re-do steps 1 through 3 and make notes on what you see. Does the water adjust its course as it reaches the mountains? Does it follow the course you thought it would?

• TAKE A GEOGRAPHY WALK
Go for a walk near a creek or river. Try to find two spots on the creek/river where the water seems to move at different speeds. At each one, measure a distance of, say, 12 feet and time how long it takes for a piece of cork or some other floating object to go the full 12 feet. Compare the speeds; were they different? If so, why?

Record your findings in your Geography Journal.

Rain Gauge
⍞ Materials: empty jar with a wide opening, plastic ruler, masking tape, and pen or Sharpie; Canada Wild

➜ INTRO
Today, we will make a simple rain gauge.

➜ DEMONSTRATE
1\. Set up your jar or can outside to collect rainwater.
2\. Place a ruler inside the jar and tape it on the outside.
3\. Use the ruler to mark 1/4 inch, 1/2 inch, 3/4 inch, and 1 inch. Continue by quarter inches up to 2 inches.
4\. You will check the rain jar daily to see how much it has rained. Make notes on a page in your geography journal about how much it rains (when it rains). Don't forget to empty the jar after you make note of the amount, so that you start again from zero.
5\. At the end of each week and month, add up how much it has rained in total.

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
Look at the Canada Precipitation Map to see how much it rains on Vancouver Island. Also, look at your area on the map. Are they similar or different? Where in Canada is there the most rain? Where is there the least?
∞ Image Link: Canada - Average Annual Precipitation

Do a drawing of the water cycle together, making sure the main points of evaporation and condensation, along with precipitation, runoff/collection, etc., are covered. Water is always moving.

3\. Orally narrate or draw a picture of what happened.

Linear Map

➜ INTRO
Last year, you made a map of your room or schoolroom. Today, we will start a map of a hall or a linear area of your home or school.

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. Choose an area to map that is in a line (referred to as linear). For example, a hall.
2\. Begin to draw your linear space in your Geography journal.
3\. Make sure to represent anything in the hall that takes up space on the floor. (any tables, bookshelves, carpet, etc.)
4\. Next lesson, we will add any doors that connect to the hall (or other linear space). This will take 2 lessons, so we will finish it next week.

Linear Map

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. We are going to continue your “Linear map” in your geography journal.
2\. Now we will add any doors that connect to the hall (or other linear space).
3\. Make sure to look back at the real space as you draw to keep things in order.
4\. When you are done, feel free to colour your map, if desired.

➜ NOTE
If the student really enjoys this, feel free to do other spaces that they are familiar with, such as a hall at a friend’s or family member’s house. It might be a fun challenge to see what they remember about a familiar house, and then to go see it and map it. Did they remember the correct order of rooms off the hall?

Linear Street Map
⍞ Materials: wooden blocks, Duplos or Legos attached to each other, or small boxes (like the size of single-serving cereal boxes), paper, pencil, or coloured pencils

➜ NOTE
This map project will take 4 lessons. The focus again is to keep things in a linear order.

➜ INTRO
We are going to make another type of “linear” map. This time, we will be using blocks with pictures taped on them to make a 3-dimensional map.

➜ MAP DRAWING
1\. Choose an area that you can see from your home/school, or very close to it. (It can be the street where you live or possibly a small strip of stores.)
2\. Last year, you may have made a map of your house and a couple of neighbours. This year, you will draw more. Decide if you want to make a map of just one side of the street or both sides.
3\. Spend several minutes really looking at the area you chose. Ask questions like...How many buildings are there? Are the buildings all the same size? If not, which ones are bigger? And how much bigger?
4\. Pay attention to the order the buildings are in.
5\. Draw a simple picture of each home/building and tape it to a block.

➜ NOTE
Next week, the student will finish drawing the homes/buildings.

Linear Street Map, cont.
⍞ Materials: wooden blocks, Duplos or Legos attached to each other, or small boxes (like the size of single-serving cereal boxes), paper, pencil, or coloured pencils

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Finish drawing the pictures of homes/buildings and taping them to the blocks.
2\. Teacher and student work together to set up the blocks/boxes to match the street.

➜ NOTE
Next lesson, you will begin drawing a map of your “street.”

Linear Street Map, cont.
⍞ Materials: blocks with pictures from the previous lesson, geography journal, pencil or coloured pencils

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Set up your street view with the blocks again.
2\. Draw in your geography journal a simple map of your street (blocks). (Note: You can choose to draw only one side of the street or both sides.)
3\. Pay attention as you draw your map. Does it match the blocks? Are they in the right order?

➜ NOTE
The drawing of the Street View Map will continue next lesson. Also, we will move the blocks and set them up again just using the map from your geography journal.

Linear Street Map Conclusion
⍞ Materials: blocks with pictures from the previous lesson, a pencil, or coloured pencils

➜ MAP DRAWING
(Street View Map)
1\. Finish drawing into your geography journal a simple map of your street (blocks). (Note: You can choose to draw only one side of the street or both sides.)
2\. Pay attention as you draw your map. Does it match the blocks? Are they in the right order?
3\. Now, move the blocks and mix them up.
4\. Only using the Street View Map that you made in your geography journal, set up the blocks in the correct order again.
5\. If time allows, go outside and see if your drawn Street View Map matches the “real thing.”

Gulfs and Bays
⍞ Materials: Globe or World Wall Map, My Map of Canada; geography journal

➜ INTRO
Ontario, and the next province we will study - Quebec - both border on Hudson Bay. For the next two lessons, we will learn more about gulfs and bays.

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
∞ Map Link: Map of North America
1\. Locate the Gulf of Alaska, Hudson Bay, and Baffin Bay.
2\. Do you notice any differences between the bays and the gulf? (The gulf is more “open” or wider.)
3\. Can you find any more gulfs or bays on this map? (the Gulf of Mexico, the Gulf of St. Lawrence, the Gulf of California, and Chesapeake Bay, which is not labelled on the map, but located near Washington, DC)
4\. Take a few minutes to look at your world wall map or globe. See if you can find other bays or gulfs.
5\. What did you notice about the differences between them? (You may notice that some bays look like gulfs, and some gulfs look like bays. Typically, bays are smaller than gulfs. Both bays and gulfs are portions of larger bodies of water that extend into the land. They also have wide mouths where they join the larger body of water, but gulfs can sometimes have narrow mouths as well, like the Persian Gulf. The Bay of Biscay, north of Spain and France, is actually a gulf.)
6\. Read the following definition: National Geographic defines a bay as “a body of water partly surrounded by land. A bay is usually smaller and less enclosed than a gulf. The mouth of the bay, where [it] meets the ocean or lake, is typically wider than that of a gulf. “

➜ MAP WORK
⍞ Map Resource: My Map of Canada
1\. Label Baffin Bay and the Gulf of St. Lawrence on your map.
2\. Draw and label any other bodies of water that are of interest, using the Map of North America as a resource.

Model of Bay
⍞ Materials: pan, water, large rock(s); Canada Wild

➜ MODEL
Before doing this demonstration, how do you think waves from the ocean are different from waves from a bay? (Ocean waves are large, and bay waves are small.)
1\. Cover the bottom of a shallow pan with water—maybe halfway up.
2\. Blow across the pan and watch the waves.
3\. Next, place a big rock or other object in the pan so that it rises above the water.
4\. Now, blow across the pan where the object is now in the waves.
5\. Does the size or choppiness of the waves differ between open water wind and obstructed wind?
6\. Add a sketch to your journal diagramming ocean waves versus bay waves.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.18-21 "Ungava Seal" - "to call home."

➜ VIEW & DISCUSS
∞ Map Link: Nunavik Map
• Ungava Seals live in Nunavik, the northern third portion of Québec. Look at this map and notice how many lakes and rivers there are. Can you find a new bay we haven't seen on the smaller-scale maps so far? (Ungava Bay)

2\. Label Québec City with a star to show it is a capital city.

Continents
⍞ Materials: Globe

➜ INTRO
When doing our map work, sometimes we have used the word "continent." Do you remember what a continent is? This lesson might be new for some students and a helpful review for others.

➜ MAP ACTIVITY
1\. Find Ontario (and Ottawa, if it is labeled).
2\. “Zoom out” and locate Canada.
3\. “Zoom out.” What do you call the area including the United States, Canada, and Mexico? (North America)

➜ MAP QUESTIONS
(questions taken from chapters on “Surface of the Earth” in Elementary Geography by Charlotte Mason)
1\. Which continents are upon the Equator? (Africa and South America)
2\. Which three continents are north of the Equator? (North America, Europe, Asia)
3\. Which two lie south of the Equator? (Australia and Antarctica)
4\. Which is the longest [pair of] continent(s) from north to south? (North and South America)
5\. What continent lies east of Europe? (Asia)
6\. What continent lies west of Europe? (North America)
7\. What continent is to the south of Europe? (Africa)
8\. Which continent(s) are quite surrounded by water? (Australia and Antarctica)
• Let’s further explore what Ms. Mason was asking about continents.
1\. Look on the globe, and locate where North America and South America are connected. (Panama/Colombia)
2\. Where are Africa and Asia connected? (Egypt/Israel)
3\. Which two continents do not have an obvious division and look like one large continent? (Europe and Asia)

3\. How many different geographical regions do we find in New Brunswick? Each one has a number and a separate colour. (There is only one: the Appalachian Region)

Scale
⍞ Materials: pencil, paper, the Grid paper (1:3 ratio)
∞ Link: Grid paper (1:3 ratio)

➜ MAP WORK
1\. We will be using a grid to reduce a simple drawing.
2\. Take the larger grid paper.
3\. Draw a simple shape onto the large grid paper. (Draw a simple house, or any basic shape.)
4\. Draw the reduced image onto the small grid based on the figure on the large grid.
5\. Share your observations.

➜ NOTE
In a few lessons, we will use this skill of scale drawing to draw a map of your neighbourhood to scale. The next lesson requires the game Battleship or a printed version. See next week to print ahead of time.

Scale, cont.
⍞ Materials: Battleship game or a Picture of Battleship

➜ MAP WORK
(Battleship)
1\. Observe a Battleship game board or the Picture of Battleship.
∞ Image Link: Picture of Battleship
2\. What similarities do you notice between the game board and a map? (grid lines on both)
3\. Play a game of Battleship. If you don't have the game, you can play this Printable Battleship version by printing out 2 copies (one for each person). You will need to cut out the “ships” in order to play.
∞ Link: Printable Battleship

➜ NOTE
The game Battleship is similar to a scaled-down grid of a battle. In other words, the ships are scaled-down versions of an actual ship; therefore, some ships are bigger than others. Next week, we will start a scale map of your neighbourhood.

Pacing and Scale Map
⍞ Materials: Making Your Own Maps, large piece of paper (can be a large piece of art paper, legal size paper, etc.)

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.8-9 "Say you want" - "Label it."

➜ PRACTICE
Spend a couple of minutes practicing pacing. This means that you practice keeping the same length/pace of steps. Take 10 steps away from your home and try to take 10 to get back to the same spot.

➜ MAP DRAWING
Today we will begin a new map of your home and your neighbours'. Your map will only be as big as your piece of paper.
1\. The book said that 25 steps are the same as an inch.
2\. Pace the outside general area of your home, and draw it on one of the middle edges of your paper. (For example, if it takes 50 paces to walk across the front of your home, that line would be 2 inches long on the paper. If the width is 25 steps, that will be one inch. Please don't worry about being super precise; we just want to begin building the idea of scale.)
3\. After you have drawn your home, begin to move outwards. (You will have another lesson to finish this, and you are free to work on it in the afternoons or during assessment week.)
4\. First, you will need to do things like pace the space between your home and the nearest neighbour. Then, pace the outside of your neighbour's house. (Make sure to get permission.)
5\. Work on it until your class time is up.

Scale Map, cont.

➜ MAP DRAWING
Today, we continue your scale map of your home and your neighbour's.
1\. Don't forget that 25 steps equal an inch on your map.
2\. Assess how far you went last lesson.
3\. Continue to move outward from your home. Work on it for the class time, trying to add at least 2 neighbours.
4\. Do you notice that scale mapping is much more time-consuming? I wonder how long geographers have to work on a map, and if there are things they learn to do it more quickly.
5\. If you want to continue your map, feel free to use the afternoon time.
